skill.name: web-search
skill.description: Runs web search, answer generation, and page extraction through Tavily and Exa apps using the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Search the web and extract page content through inference.sh, Tavily, and Exa.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, URL, and weak-crypto patterns, but the reviewed evidence is mostly Markdown examples and documentation links. The real risk is that the skill authorizes Bash(infsh *) and sends searches or URLs to inference.sh, Tavily, and Exa services. No prompt injection, credential theft, destructive command, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Broad inference.sh CLI Permission
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(infsh *), which lets the assistant run any inference.sh CLI subcommand. This is constrained to the infsh binary and matches the skill purpose, but it can act through the user's authenticated inference.sh account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and grants a wildcard over infsh commands. Risk is moderate because it is limited to one CLI, but account-scoped side effects depend on that CLI.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Web Search and Extraction
medium_findings.description: The examples send queries and URLs to remote inference.sh apps backed by Tavily and Exa. This is expected for a search skill, but users may expose sensitive queries, private URLs, or extracted content to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents remote search and extraction workflows. The behavior is legitimate, but the privacy exposure is clear from the Tavily, Exa, and inference.sh usage.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution alerts are false positives. The cited lines are Markdown code fences, app identifiers, table entries, or shell examples for using infsh, not Ruby code executed by the skill file itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every cited location is inside SKILL.md documentation rather than executable Ruby. The skill still has CLI risk through allowed-tools, which is tracked separately.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Detections Are Expected Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alerts are documentation, image, and example target URLs. They are expected for a web search skill and do not show hidden callback endpoints or credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are visible Markdown links or example extraction targets. No evidence found of covert network destinations or secret collection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts do not correspond to crypto operations in the reviewed Markdown. The cited lines contain frontmatter text, table formatting, or a documentation URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No hash function, cipher selection, or cryptographic API appears at the cited locations. These detections appear to be keyword matches in prose or Markdown syntax.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard CLI Tool Allowance
dangerous_patterns.description: Bash(infsh *) is a broad command pattern. It is not malicious by itself, but marketplace users should understand that the skill can invoke remote inference.sh app workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard permission is directly visible in the tool declaration. The risk depends on what the installed infsh CLI and account can access.

content.value_statement: Current web research often requires switching between search, extraction, and summarization tools. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a direct workflow for search results, source extraction, and research inputs through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Runs Tavily Search Assistant queries through the inference.sh CLI.
Extracts clean text and images from URLs with Tavily Extract.
Runs Exa search for relevant links and contextual results.
Uses Exa Answer for direct factual answers from web sources.
Combines search or extraction outputs with Claude model workflows.
Lists related inference.sh skills for broader agent workflows.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated account.
Depends on Tavily, Exa, and inference.sh service availability.
Search quality depends on query wording and provider coverage.
Users should avoid sending sensitive URLs or private content to remote services.

use_cases.title: Source-backed research briefs
use_cases.description: Gather current web sources, extract relevant pages, and prepare material for a concise research summary.

use_cases.title: RAG input collection
use_cases.description: Find and extract web content that can be reviewed before adding it to retrieval pipelines.

use_cases.title: Claim verification workflows
use_cases.description: Search for supporting sources and extract pages that help evaluate a specific factual claim.

prompt_templates.title: Search a topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the web for recent, reliable sources about [topic]. Return the most useful findings with source links.

prompt_templates.title: Extract a page
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the main content from [URL]. Summarize the key points and note any images or source metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Compare sources
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for sources about [claim]. Compare the results and identify agreements, conflicts, and missing evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Build a research pack
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for [topic], extract the top sources, and prepare a structured research pack for a Claude analysis workflow.

output_examples.input: Search for current best practices in AI agent evaluation.
output_examples.output: A ranked set of source links, concise findings, and notes about which sources are most relevant for evaluation methods.

output_examples.input: Extract a long technical article from a public URL.
output_examples.output: Clean article text, a short summary, key sections, and available image references from the page.

output_examples.input: Check whether a product claim is supported by public sources.
output_examples.output: Search results grouped by supporting, conflicting, and inconclusive evidence, with source links for review.

best_practices: Use precise queries and include date ranges when freshness matters.
Review extracted sources before using them in reports or RAG pipelines.
Keep sensitive internal URLs, private documents, and credentials out of search inputs.
anti_patterns: Do not treat search results as verified facts without source review.
Do not send private customer data to third-party extraction services.
Do not rely on one provider when the task needs high-confidence coverage.

faq.question: Does this skill require an external account?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires the inference.sh CLI and login before the documented commands can run.

faq.question: Which search providers does it use?
faq.answer: The documented workflows use Tavily and Exa apps through inference.sh.

faq.question: Can it extract content from any URL?
faq.answer: It can request extraction for public URLs, but access and quality depend on provider limits and page restrictions.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential research?
faq.answer: Use caution. Queries, URLs, and extracted content may be sent to external services.

faq.question: Can it support RAG workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It can collect and extract source material that users can review before adding to retrieval systems.

faq.question: Does it verify facts automatically?
faq.answer: No. It helps collect sources and answers, but users should verify important claims before publishing or acting.
